memories.content: @echecs/tournament is a TypeScript package for managing chess tournaments, part of the echecsjs organization. Version 3.0.0 involved a major type redesign.
@echecs/trf is a package that depends on @echecs/tournament — specifically trf#24 depends on tournament#20 (the type redesign).
In @echecs/tournament v3.0.0, Game is modeled as a discriminated union with forfeit/rated/string results instead of the previous kind/numeric result approach.
@echecs/tournament v3.0.0 removed GameKind, numeric Result, TournamentOptions, TournamentSnapshot, and PairingResult types.

@echecs/tournament v3.0.0 added CompletedRound, Round, Pairings, NationalRating, ScoringSystem, TournamentData, TournamentMetadata, Team, PointAdjustment, AcceleratedRound, PlayerAcceleration, and ProhibitedPairing types.
FIDE_SCORING is a constant representing the standard FIDE scoring system for chess: win=1, draw=0.5, loss=0.
Bye in @echecs/tournament has kind: 'full' | 'half' | 'pairing' | 'zero', replacing the previous simpler bye model.
Chess tournament byes come in different types: full bye (1 point), half bye (0.5 points), pairing bye (given when odd number of players), and zero bye (0 points).
In chess tournaments, a game can be a forfeit (unplayed win/loss), a rated game (played with a chess result), or have other special results.
echecsjs is the GitHub organization hosting chess-related TypeScript packages including @echecs/tournament and @echecs/trf.
@echecs/tournament uses a ScoringSystem type to define how points are awarded for wins, draws, and losses — making scoring configurable rather than hardcoded.
@echecs/tournament models rounds as either CompletedRound or Round, and pairings as a separate Pairings type.
NationalRating is a type in @echecs/tournament representing a player's national federation rating, distinct from their FIDE rating.
@echecs/tournament v3.0.0 supports Teams, PointAdjustments, AcceleratedRounds, PlayerAcceleration, and ProhibitedPairings as first-class types.
Player type in @echecs/tournament requires points and rank fields.
bakuAcceleration is a function in @echecs/tournament that implements Baku acceleration for Swiss-system tournaments. It requires players to have points and rank data.
Baku acceleration is a method used in Swiss-system chess tournaments to modify pairings in early rounds, helping to separate players of different strength levels more quickly.
@echecs/tournament uses pnpm as package manager with lint, test, and build scripts for verification.
@echecs/tournament uses eqeqeq lint rule — strict equality (!== undefined) instead of loose equality (!= null).
@echecs/tournament uses discriminated unions for Game types rather than enum-based approaches, enabling better TypeScript type narrowing.

@echecs/trf currently defines its own Tournament, Player, RoundResult types in src/types.ts. After tournament#20 lands, trf#24 should import those types from @echecs/tournament instead of duplicating them.

The release strategy for echecsjs type redesign: merge and publish @echecs/tournament v3.0.0 first, then do @echecs/trf changes (trf#24) as a follow-up that depends on the published tournament types. Avoids cross-repo blocking and huge PRs.

@echecs/trf is a TRF (Tournament Report File) parser/serializer package. It has its own src/types.ts with Tournament, Player, RoundResult types that will be replaced by imports from @echecs/tournament after v3.0.0.
PR #32 on echecsjs/tournament implemented the type redesign from issue #20. It was merged, enabling v3.0.0 release so trf#24 can depend on published types.
echecsjs/tournament has pre-push hooks that run lint/test/build checks before allowing pushes.
@echecs/tournament v3.0.0 was published after PR #32 merged, unblocking trf#24 which depends on the new tournament types.

trf#24 core change: parse() returns TournamentData (from @echecs/tournament) instead of trf's own Tournament type, and stringify() consumes TournamentData. Per-player RoundResult[] replaced by CompletedRound[] with Game[].
In trf#24, TRF's Tournament extends TournamentData — parse returns a superset with TRF-specific fields (version, abnormalPoints, team encoding records, etc.).
In trf#24 round construction: parse builds CompletedRound[] by cross-referencing all players' per-round entries. The white player's line is canonical for game data.
In trf#24 pairing number mapping: id = String(pairingNumber), startingRank = pairingNumber. stringify() reverses this mapping.

@echecs/trf parse.ts (~800+ lines) parses TRF format text into structured tournament data. stringify.ts reconstructs TRF text from tournament data. Combined ~1600 lines of source with ~3000 lines of tests (369 tests).
TRF (Tournament Report File) is a standard format for chess tournament data exchange. Each player line contains per-round results that need to be cross-referenced to build complete round/game data.
trf#24 plan was saved to docs/plans/2026-05-09-couple-tournament-types.md with 7 tasks. Implementation approach: types first, then parse, then stringify, then tests.
In TRF parsing, the white player's line is treated as canonical for game data when cross-referencing per-round entries from both sides of a pairing.
@echecs/tournament v3.0.0 was auto-released via CI release workflow after the version bump in PR #32 was merged.

For trf#24 implementation: tasks 2-4 (types, parse, stringify, index) were tightly coupled and dispatched as one subagent rather than separate subagents per task — changing types/parse/stringify independently would cause cascading type errors. Tests (task 6) dispatched separately after.

When refactoring tightly coupled type/parse/stringify code across echecsjs packages, it's better to rewrite them as a unit rather than incrementally — individual changes cause cascading type errors until all pieces are updated.

@echecs/trf uses unicorn/no-null ESLint rule — all null usages require eslint-disable comments. When prettier reformats multi-line expressions, eslint-disable-next-line comments can end up targeting the wrong line — use eslint-disable-line on the actual null line instead.
In @echecs/trf, ResultCode is internal only — it must not be exported from index.ts. The issue spec explicitly requires this.

@echecs/trf uses lint-staged with prettier in pre-commit hooks. Prettier reformats staged files which can shift line numbers, causing eslint-disable-next-line comments to target wrong lines. Always verify lint after prettier formatting.

In @echecs/trf parse.ts, the round building logic cross-references player entries: for 001 lines it builds games from both players' perspectives, for 801/802 team lines it uses opponentId with Number(oppRaw) || null pattern.

TRF line types: 001 = player data with per-round results, 012 = tournament name, 022 = city, 032 = federation, 042 = dates, 052 = chief arbiter, 062 = deputy arbiters, 072 = number of rounds, 082 = number of players, 092 = number of rated players, 102 = number of teams, 132 = time control. 300 = team compositions, 320 = team round data. 801/802 = alternative team result formats.

trf#24 implementation complete: 371 tests pass (up from 369), lint clean, build clean. Source rewrite and test rewrite committed separately. Plan file was removed after completion as a working artifact.
trf#24 implementation finished locally — lint, 371 tests, build all passing. Ready for PR but not yet pushed or opened as of end of session.
bbpPairings is a pairing engine used as a reference implementation for Swiss tournament pairings
@echecs/swiss is the codebase's own Swiss pairing implementation, compared against bbpPairings for correctness
204-player tournaments cause differences between bbpPairings and the @echecs/swiss implementation — this is a known edge case requiring debugging
RTG (Random Tournament Generator) is used to generate test tournaments with specific seeds and player counts for comparison testing between pairing engines
Swiss pairing system is a tournament format where players are paired based on score groups and color preferences, governed by FIDE rules
TRF format uses '001' prefix for player rows — the format encodes tournament data on structured lines with specific line-type prefixes

RTG (Random Tournament Generator) accepts a seed parameter and a config-file option via `-g` flag — player count varies by seed, and specific seeds need to be found or configured to produce a desired player count like 204
dutch.ts contains the main Dutch pairing algorithm implementation — includes edge weight computation and a bracket loop that iterates through score groups to produce pairings
matching-computer.ts implements the matching graph computation used by the Dutch pairing algorithm — critical for finding optimal pairings within score brackets
dynamic-uint is a data structure used in the Swiss pairing implementation — likely for handling large integer arithmetic needed in edge weight computation
TRF files generated by bbpPairings RTG use CR-only (\r) line endings instead of LF (\n) — this causes wc -l to report 0 lines and requires special handling when parsing
bbpPairings RTG player count is determined by the random seed — to find tournaments with a specific player count (like 204), you need to scan multiple seeds and check the output

The stale baseVertexMatch bug in matching-computer.ts setEdgeWeight: C++ only calls prepareVertexForWeightAdjustments on the modified vertex (single-prepare), but a previous fix (commit e0ec62c) added double-prepare (preparing the neighbor too) to fix stale baseVertexMatch pointers. Double-prepare fixes early-round stale state bugs but causes later-round over-preparation divergence from bbpPairings.

RootBlossom constructor bug: in freeAncestorOfBase, new RootBlossoms were created with Label.FREE (3) instead of LABEL_ZERO (1) as in C++, and minOuterEdges was initialized to empty array instead of being sized to match the old rootBlossom's minOuterEdges size. Both fixes were needed to match C++ behavior.

Two separate bugs in the matching computer: (1) stale state from prior matchings causing wrong pairings in early rounds — fixed by double-prepare but over-corrects later rounds, (2) RootBlossom constructor in freeAncestorOfBase using wrong label (FREE vs ZERO) and empty minOuterEdges — these are C++ port translation errors

Seed 4 (115 players) divergence results table: double-prepare + old constructor = rounds 3/4/6 PERFECT, round 11 FAIL. Single-prepare + old constructor = rounds 3/4/6 FAIL, round 11 FAIL. Double-prepare + fixed constructor (minOuterEdges + LABEL_ZERO) = rounds 1-10 PERFECT, round 11 still FAIL. Single-prepare + fixed constructor = rounds 3/4/6 FAIL again.

finalizePairMC zeroes all edges for paired vertices v1 and v2 then sets their mutual edge to 1 — each setEdgeWeight call triggers prepareVertexForWeightAdjustments which dissolves blossoms and resets duals, and in double-prepare mode this over-prepares neighbor vertices causing downstream matching differences

src/blossom.ts is the old blossom algorithm (van Rantwijk) used for the feasibility pass — completely separate from matching/blossom.ts which is the C++ port used by MatchingComputer. The feasibility pass determines byeAssigneeScore.

With double-prepare + constructor fixes (minOuterEdges + LABEL_ZERO), seed 5 (204 players) showed rounds 1-3 PERFECT — significant improvement from original code where divergence started at round 7. Testing still in progress for later rounds.

scoreGroupSizeBits for 204 players = ceil(log2(205)) = 8 bits — larger than typical 6-7 bits for smaller tournaments. With 15 rounds and many criteria (C6-C21), edge weights can push DynamicUint into 5+ words (160+ bits).

RootBlossom constructor fixes (LABEL_ZERO + minOuterEdges sizing) helped seed 4 (115 players) but NOT seed 5 (204 players). Seed 5 still diverges from round 7 with identical results — the 204-player divergence is caused by double-prepare over-correction, not the constructor bugs. Larger tournaments with more bracket transitions are more affected by the over-preparation of dual variables.
vitest in the @echecs/swiss project can be slow to compile — 3 minutes may not be enough for the initial compilation phase. Need longer timeouts (5+ minutes) for full test runs.

The LABEL_ZERO change in freeAncestorOfBase RootBlossom constructor (changing from Label.FREE to LABEL_ZERO/1) or the minOuterEdges sizing fix may cause infinite loops in the matching algorithm — tests hang indefinitely when both are applied together. Need to isolate which change causes the hang.

The minOuterEdges sizing fix alone (without LABEL_ZERO) also causes tests to hang indefinitely — the minOuterEdges initialization change in the RootBlossom constructor is the problematic change, not LABEL_ZERO. Must be reverted.

Summary of debugging session findings for 204-player tournament divergence:

Two C++ port bugs found in RootBlossom constructor (src/matching/blossom.ts:220-238):
1. label set to Label.FREE (3) instead of LABEL_ZERO (1) — affects how dissolved blossoms are treated during augmentation
2. minOuterEdges initialized to empty [] instead of sized from old rootBlossom — BUT this fix causes infinite loops

Pre-existing workaround: setEdgeWeight double-prepare (commit e0ec62c) — prepares both vertices instead of just the modified one like C++. Fixes stale baseVertexMatch bugs but over-corrects by resetting too many dual variables, causing divergence in larger tournaments.

The minOuterEdges fix can't simply copy C++ behavior without causing hangs — the augmentation loop depends on minOuterEdges state in ways that differ between the TS port and C++ original. Needs deeper investigation.

Confirmed: the minOuterEdges sizing fix in the RootBlossom constructor definitively causes infinite loops in the matching algorithm. This is not an orphaned process issue — the fix itself creates a loop. The C++ minOuterEdges initialization pattern cannot be directly ported to the TypeScript implementation without deeper changes to the augmentation logic.

vitest hang was caused by dutch.fixtures.spec.ts — specifically the issue_15 fixture (180 players, 11 rounds) takes extremely long. Running tests excluding that fixture: 14 files, 250 tests pass in 331ms. The fixture-based tests are the slow ones for large tournaments.

issue_15 fixture (180 players, 11 rounds) takes 17-27 seconds per round in the original code — ~275 seconds total. This is an inherent performance issue with large tournaments, not caused by any bug fix. Tests need 10+ minute timeouts to complete.

issue_15 fixture (180 players) rounds 6, 8, 10, 11 fail with vitest's 30-second individual test timeout. Each of these rounds takes ~30s to compute pairings. Need to check whether this is a regression from the fixes or pre-existing performance issue.

Confirmed: issue_15 fixture timeouts are pre-existing — original code also fails rounds 6, 8, 11 with 30s timeout. The constructor fixes (LABEL_ZERO + minOuterEdges) do NOT cause the timeouts. Final diff is only matching/blossom.ts with both constructor fixes applied, double-prepare kept as-is in matching-computer.ts.

Final isolation: minOuterEdges sizing fix alone is sufficient — LABEL_ZERO change is unnecessary. With just minOuterEdges fix + double-prepare: seed 4 rounds 1-10 and 12 PERFECT, only round 11 differs. The LABEL_ZERO change added no improvement.

Complete debugging session findings for 204-player tournament divergence:

FIXED: minOuterEdges constructor bug in RootBlossom (blossom.ts:232) — empty array instead of sized from old rootBlossom. Improves seed 4 from 8/12 to 11/12 rounds matching bbpPairings.

NOT FIXED: double-prepare in setEdgeWeight (matching-computer.ts) — prepares both vertices instead of only the modified vertex like C++. This is the root cause of remaining divergence for both seed 4 round 11 and seed 5 (204 players) rounds 7-15. Cannot simply revert to single-prepare because that reintroduces the stale baseVertexMatch bug from commit e0ec62c.

ROOT CAUSE: the double-prepare over-corrects by resetting dual variables and dissolving blossoms on the neighbor vertex. This changes the warm-start state that the blossom algorithm relies on, producing different (but still valid) maximum weight matchings. The effect scales with tournament size — more bracket transitions = more accumulated divergence.

NEXT STEPS NEEDED: investigate why C++ single-prepare doesn't have the stale baseVertexMatch issue. The answer likely lies in subtle differences in how the TS port handles blossom dissolution, vertex labeling, or match pointer cleanup during prepareVertexForWeightAdjustments.

Minimal 8-vertex reproduction of single-prepare vs double-prepare divergence: single-prepare produces different matchings across phases based on accumulated blossom state (warm-start), while double-prepare always produces the same trivial matching (adjacent pairs) because it resets too much state. C++ uses single-prepare and relies on warm-start for efficiency — the accumulated state is intentional, not a bug.

After prepareVertexForWeightAdjustments: prepared vertex has baseVertexMatch=undefined, dualVariable=aboveMaxEdgeWeight>>1 (positive) so labeled OUTER. Partner vertex has baseVertexMatch=undefined (cleared by line 256), but stale dualVariable from previous matching. If dual is zero → ZERO label, if positive → OUTER label. The partner's blossom structure is NOT dissolved with single-prepare.

Seed 4 round 3 divergence with single-prepare: bye goes to player 101 instead of expected 113. All 6 wrong pairings involve bottom-of-table players 82-113 in score 0-0.5 brackets. The bye assignee is determined by the bracket loop pairing, not the feasibility pass — so the matching difference cascades to affect bye assignment.

Critical blossom port bug found (Bug A): Vertex.minOuterEdgeResistance initialized to zero (vertex.ts:99) instead of aboveMaxEdgeWeight (infinity sentinel) as in C++. This corrupts inner-outer edge tracking — every vertex starts thinking it has a zero-resistance outer edge, so real edges never replace it. This is likely the PRIMARY root cause of the stale state issue that necessitated the double-prepare workaround.

Three critical correctness bugs found in blossom port audit:
Bug A: Vertex.minOuterEdgeResistance initialized to zero instead of aboveMaxEdgeWeight (vertex.ts:99) — PRIMARY root cause
Bug B: RootBlossom constructor label set to FREE instead of ZERO (blossom.ts) — already investigated, LABEL_ZERO change not independently helpful
Bug C: (from audit results, details in later chunks)

Bug C (connectChildren first child parentBlossom) was a FALSE POSITIVE from the audit. Both C++ and TS intentionally leave first child's parentBlossom as nullptr/undefined in connectChildren — it gets set at graph.ts:1025 by the caller formBlossomFromPath.

Bug A (Vertex.minOuterEdgeResistance initialized to zero) is a correct fix for code hygiene but does NOT change runtime behavior. The initializeInnerOuterEdges function at line 856 resets minOuterEdgeResistance to aboveMaxEdgeWeight before scanning, overwriting the constructor value. This fix alone does NOT enable single-prepare.

Two commits made from the blossom audit:
1. 958dc6d — RootBlossom minOuterEdges sized from old root blossom instead of empty array
2. 6980e83 — RootBlossom label LABEL_ZERO instead of FREE, Vertex.minOuterEdgeResistance initialized to aboveMaxEdgeWeight, setEdgeWeight comment documenting double-prepare workaround

Current state after fixes: seed 4 = 11/12 rounds perfect (round 11 still differs from double-prepare). Seed 5 (204 players) unchanged — rounds 7-15 still diverge. The root cause of needing double-prepare remains unidentified.

Critical finding for round 11 divergence: the matching computer operates on a GLOBAL graph of all 115 vertices. Our matching (4-15, 1-2, 9-8) actually has HIGHER total weight (20.3e96) than bbpPairings' matching (4-9, 1-15) at 13.6e96. The blossom algorithm found a BETTER matching — 3 cross-bracket pairs vs 2 within-bracket pairs. But bbpPairings produces 2 pairs because its warm-started blossom state (from single-prepare) guides it to a different augmentation path. Double-prepare resets that state, allowing the algorithm to find the globally heavier matching which doesn't match bbpPairings.

The VERY FIRST computeMatching call (bracket-initial for top score group) already diverges between single-prepare and double-prepare. No prior bracket state to be stale — divergence comes from the initialization phase. With 115 players (odd), 6555 setEdgeWeight calls happen during feasibility weight initialization. Double-prepare gives ALL vertices the same dual value. Single-prepare leaves some vertices (e.g., vertex 0) with dual=0 because they're never the modifiedVertex argument. This dual difference changes augmentation paths.

The initialization loop in dutch.ts lines 691-707 iterates with inner loop for opponentIndex < playerIndex. With single-prepare (C++ behavior), playerIndex=0 has no iterations so vertex 0 is never prepared as modifiedVertex. With double-prepare, vertex 0 gets prepared as neighbor in setEdgeWeight(1, 0, w). This asymmetry in vertex preparation during init is the source of the first matching divergence.

Round 11 divergence root cause: tiebreaking difference in blossom algorithm augmentation paths. Both matchings are maximum-weight (ours actually has HIGHER total weight with 3 pairs vs 2 pairs). The FIDE Dutch system specifies a deterministic algorithm, so matching should be unique given same edge weights. The double-prepare creates uniform duals across all vertices during initialization, while C++ single-prepare leaves vertex 0 with dual=0, creating asymmetry that guides augmentation to bbpPairings' expected result. The remaining question: is the edge weight computation identical to bbpPairings, or does a weight difference cause the mismatch?

previousBlossom fix: changed freeAncestorOfBase to use rootChild.previousBlossom (O(1) like C++) instead of walking the entire cycle (O(k)). Functionally correct but does NOT fix the single-prepare vs double-prepare divergence. Committed as a separate correctness fix.

Phase 2 hypothesis for round 11 divergence: the issue is NOT in tiebreaking — our matching has genuinely higher weight (3 pairs vs 2 pairs). The blossom algorithm finds a more globally optimal matching that pairs cross-bracket players, while bbpPairings' warm-started state constrains it to within-bracket pairs. The remaining investigation should focus on whether there's a subtle difference in how edge weights are set or how the matching computer accumulates state across the initialization loop that causes this behavioral divergence.

Minimal 4-vertex all-equal-weight graph reproduction: with single-prepare, vertex 0 has dual=0 while v1-v3 have dual=200. After initializeInnerOuterEdges, v0 is labeled ZERO, v1-v3 are labeled OUTER. The ZERO-OUTER augmentation triggers with resistance(v0,v1)=198, causing augmentation path through v0-v1. With double-prepare, all duals are 200, all vertices start as OUTER, and a different augmentation sequence occurs.

Critical edge weight indexing difference between C++ and TS: C++ Vertex::resistance uses this.edgeWeights[that.vertexIndex] (indexing from the calling vertex's perspective), while TS resistanceInto(out, u, v) uses u.edgeWeights[v.vertexIndex]. In initializeInnerOuterEdges, C++ calls outerVertex.resistance(_, innerVertex) but TS calls resistanceInto(_, innerVertex, outerVertex) — the edge weight lookup is from different vertex perspectives. Since edge weights are symmetric (set on both sides in setEdgeWeight), this should be functionally equivalent, but worth verifying.

DEFINITIVE ROOT CAUSE IDENTIFIED: the augmentMatching implementation in graph.ts produces different augmenting paths than C++ graph.cpp given IDENTICAL vertex duals and edge weights. Proven by replaying bbpPairings' exact SET/MATCH sequence through our MatchingComputer — the very first MATCH call gives a different matching. A persistent MC (with accumulated state from SET calls) diverges from a fresh MC even though edge weights are identical. The bug is in the blossom algorithm's augmentation logic, not in edge weight computation or the double-prepare workaround.

Breakthrough methodology: captured bbpPairings' debug output (34,814 lines showing INIT/ADD/SET/MATCH operations) by running bbpPairings with a truncated 2-round TRF and pairing round 3. Replayed the exact same operation sequence through the TS MatchingComputer to prove the algorithm diverges from C++. The debug output uses hex-encoded DynamicUint edge weights.

bbpPairings paired output format: first line is count (e.g., 58 = 57 pairs + 1 bye), then one pair per line as "white black". The last line "113 0" means player 113 gets the bye (opponent 0 = no opponent).

Three commits made so far in the investigation:
1. 958dc6d — minOuterEdges sizing in RootBlossom constructor
2. 6980e83 — LABEL_ZERO + minOuterEdgeResistance + setEdgeWeight comment
3. (commit hash TBD) — previousBlossom O(1) fix in freeAncestorOfBase

All are correct C++ port fixes but NONE fix the fundamental augmentMatching divergence. The remaining investigation requires line-by-line comparison of graph.ts augmentMatching vs C++ graph.cpp.

DEFINITIVE ROOT CAUSE OF 204-PLAYER DIVERGENCE: the rootBlossoms array iteration order diverges from C++'s IterablePool iteration order after blossom creation/destruction cycles. This causes tiebreaking differences in augmentMatching when multiple vertices have the same resistance/dual values.

C++ uses IterablePool — a memory pool with LIFO free list reuse and doubly-linked list iteration (new elements appended to tail, destroyed elements return slot to free list head). The iteration order depends on allocation history.

TS uses a plain Array with push() to append and splice() to remove. After many blossom creation/destruction cycles, the array order diverges significantly from the pool iteration order.

FIX NEEDED: replace Array<RootBlossom> with an IterablePool implementation matching C++ semantics — fixed-capacity pre-allocated slots, LIFO free list, doubly-linked iteration list with hide()/unhide() support.

C++ IterablePool semantics for rootBlossomPool:
- construct(): takes from free list head (LIFO), appends to END of iteration list (tail)
- hide(): removes from iteration list but keeps alive (for temporary removal during edge consolidation)
- unhide(): re-inserts at original position in iteration list
- destroy(): removes from iteration list, returns slot to free list head (LIFO)
- begin()/end(): iterate only visible (non-hidden) elements via forward links
- initializeFromChildren(): hides all originals, consolidates edges, destroys originals

Evidence of rootBlossoms order divergence: after MATCH #1 and 7258 SET calls, rootBlossoms array has vertices in scrambled order — v93 at [0], v94 at [1], ..., v114 at [21], then v1 at [22], v79 at [23], etc., with v0 at position [110]. This scrambled order results from push/splice operations during blossom dissolution and creation, and does NOT match C++ pool iteration order.

Both matchings (ours vs bbpPairings) have IDENTICAL total weight — confirmed as maximum-weight matchings. The divergence is purely tiebreaking — when multiple augmenting paths have equal resistance, the iteration order of rootBlossoms determines which path is chosen. Both results are mathematically valid but FIDE Dutch pairing requires deterministic output matching bbpPairings.

Implementation plan for IterablePool to fix rootBlossoms iteration order divergence:

1. Create IterablePool<T> class in src/matching/iterable-pool.ts matching C++ semantics:
   - Fixed-capacity pre-allocated slots with LIFO free list
   - Doubly-linked iteration list (construct appends to tail)
   - hide()/unhide() for temporary removal during edge consolidation
   - destroy() returns slot to free list head
   - Symbol.iterator for for-of loops

2. Replace Array<RootBlossom> with IterablePool<RootBlossom> in graph.ts:
   - rootBlossoms: Array → IterablePool (capacity = 2*numVertices)
   - Replace push() with construct()
   - Replace splice() with destroy()
   - Replace for-of with pool iteration

3. Replace Array<ParentBlossom> similarly (lower priority)

4. Remove rootBlossomMinOuterEdgeResistances parallel array — it's vestigial (only pushed to, never read). minOuterEdgeResistance is stored directly on RootBlossom.

5. Revert setEdgeWeight to single-prepare (C++ behavior) after pool is implemented

6. Verify: seed 4 should be 12/12 perfect, seed 5 (204 players) should match bbpPairings

rootBlossomMinOuterEdgeResistances parallel array in graph.ts is vestigial — only pushed to during addVertex, never read. The actual minOuterEdgeResistance value is stored directly on the RootBlossom object. Can be safely removed during IterablePool refactor.
@echecs/swiss is a package that contains tiebreak calculation functions for Swiss tournaments
@echecs/tournament is a package in the echecs ecosystem that shares the Tiebreak function signature with @echecs/swiss
Buchholz is a tiebreaker used in Swiss chess tournaments — it sums the scores of all opponents a player has faced
Buchholz Cut is a tiebreaker variant that removes the lowest-scoring opponent before summing Buchholz scores
Median Buchholz is a tiebreaker variant that removes both the highest and lowest opponent scores before summing
Sonneborn-Berger is a chess tiebreaker that sums the scores of opponents weighted by the result against each opponent
Progressive score is a chess tiebreaker that sums cumulative round-by-round scores
Direct encounter is a chess tiebreaker based on the head-to-head result between tied players
Tiebreak functions in the echecs codebase follow the signature: (playerId: string, players: Player[], games: Game[]) => number
The echecs codebase has 6 tiebreak functions: buchholz, buchholzCut, medianBuchholz, sonnebornBerger, progressive, directEncounter
Each tiebreaker in the echecs ecosystem should be its own independent package — one package per tiebreak, not grouped together
FIDE C.07 is the regulation document that defines official tiebreak rules for chess tournaments, effective March 2026
FIDE C.07 Section 8 covers Buchholz tiebreakers including standard Buchholz, Average Opponents Buchholz, and Fore-Buchholz variants
Koya is a chess tiebreaker defined in FIDE C.07 Section 9.2 — it measures performance against opponents who scored 50% or more
Number of wins is a chess tiebreaker counting total wins achieved by a player in a tournament
Average Opponents Buchholz (AOB) is a Buchholz variant from FIDE C.07 Section 8
Fore-Buchholz is a Buchholz variant from FIDE C.07 Section 8 that considers only games played before a certain round

Recommended package structure groups tiebreakers by FIDE family: @echecs/buchholz (Section 8, multiple exports), @echecs/sonneborn-berger (Section 9.1), @echecs/koya (Section 9.2), @echecs/progressive (Section 7.5), @echecs/direct-encounter (Section 6), @echecs/number-of-wins
All tiebreak packages in the echecs ecosystem use an identical Tiebreak function signature — no variation between packages
Tiebreak packages keep types (Game, Player, Result, Tiebreak) local to each package rather than sharing a types dependency — follows the zero-dependency standalone pattern of the monorepo
The echecs monorepo follows a zero-dependency pattern where each package is fully standalone and does not depend on other packages in the monorepo
Game, Player, Result, and Tiebreak are shared type definitions used across tiebreak packages — currently duplicated between swiss/src/types.ts and tournament/src/types.ts

@echecs/buchholz package exports three functions: buchholz (FIDE 8.1 — sum of opponents' scores), averageOpponentsBuchholz (FIDE 8.2), and foreBuchholz (FIDE 8.3 — Buchholz as if final round games were draws)
FIDE C.07 Section 14 defines modifiers for tiebreakers — these are handled separately from the core tiebreak functions

Each tiebreak package in the echecs monorepo follows a standard file layout: src/index.ts (public API barrel), src/types.ts (Game, Player, Result, Tiebreak), src/utilities.ts (internal helpers: score, opponentIds, gamesForPlayer), src/__tests__/index.spec.ts, plus config files (package.json, tsconfig.json, tsdown.config.ts, eslint.config.mjs, prettier.config.mjs, .prettierignore)
Tiebreak packages share common internal utility functions: score (calculates player score), opponentIds (gets list of opponent IDs), gamesForPlayer (filters games involving a player)

Migration plan for tiebreak packages: Phase 1 — create 8 standalone tiebreak packages. Phase 2 (future) — @echecs/swiss replaces internal tiebreaks.ts with imports from new packages, existing 6 functions become re-exports (minor version bump). Phase 3 (future) — @echecs/tournament does the same migration.

The Tiebreak signature (playerId, players, games) => number exists so that any tiebreak can be called uniformly by the standings() function — standings() accepts an array of Tiebreak functions and calls each one in sequence to break ties, regardless of which data each tiebreak actually needs
standings() is a function in both @echecs/swiss and @echecs/tournament that accepts an array of Tiebreak functions and calls each in sequence to break ties between players

Direct encounter tiebreak (FIDE 6) needs the full player list to identify which players are tied — it counts points scored only in games against opponents with the same final score, so it must compute all player scores to find tied players

Every tiebreak function needs playerId and games as common parameters. The players parameter is only needed by directEncounter (to find tied players) and rating-based tiebreaks (averageRatingOfOpponents, averageRatingOfOpponentsCut1, and four performance-rating functions — to look up opponent ratings). All other tiebreaks ignore the players parameter.

The Tiebreak function signature was reordered to (playerId: string, games: Game[], players: Player[]) => number — players moved to last position since it's the least universally needed parameter. This is a breaking change but acceptable since packages are unpublished (all at 0.1.0).

TypeScript allows functions with fewer parameters to satisfy a type with more parameters — so tiebreak implementations that don't need players simply don't declare it in their parameter list while still conforming to the Tiebreak type. The caller always passes all three arguments.

FIDE handbook keeps pairing rules (C.04) and tiebreak rules (C.07) as completely separate chapters. Swiss pairing algorithms (Dutch, Dubov, Burstein, Lim) never reference any tiebreaker — they pair based on score groups, color history, and float rules only. Tiebreakers are applied after the tournament to break ties in final standings.

@echecs/swiss should drop src/tiebreaks.ts entirely — standings() receives tiebreaks as a parameter and doesn't import any specific tiebreak function. The 6 tiebreakers were just a convenience re-export. Swiss should re-export the new tiebreak packages with old names aliased for backward compatibility.
@echecs/swiss standings() function needs its Tiebreak type signature updated to (playerId, games, players) and call sites must match the new parameter order

standings() should be moved out of pairing packages (swiss, round-robin) and into @echecs/tournament — standings is a tiebreak consumer, not a pairing concern. Tournament is the orchestrator that combines pairings + tiebreaks + standings.

@echecs/swiss should be refactored to expose only pairing functions: dutch(), dubov(), burstein(), lim(), doubleSwiss(), swissTeam() — with a default export for the most common one. All tiebreaks and standings() removed.
@echecs/round-robin is a pairing package that currently exports roundRobin() + schedule() + 6 types — should be refactored to expose just a pair function
@echecs/swiss currently exports 6 pairing functions (dutch, dubov, burstein, lim, doubleSwiss, swissTeam) + 6 tiebreaks + standings() + 8 types

@echecs/swiss uses subpath exports — import pair from '@echecs/swiss' gives Dutch (the default/most common), and each variant (dubov, burstein, lim, etc.) gets its own subpath like '@echecs/swiss/dubov'

Every swiss pair function has the same signature — all subpath exports (dutch, dubov, burstein, lim, double, team) export a single `pair` function with an identical API. The default '@echecs/swiss' export maps to Dutch pairing.
The echecs pairing packages follow a subpath export pattern where each entrypoint exports a single `pair` function with a consistent signature, regardless of the underlying algorithm
Game interface in the echecs ecosystem: { blackId: string; result: Result; round: number; whiteId: string } — the round field indicates which round the game was played in

The games parameter passed to pair() and tiebreak functions is the full array of all games played in the tournament so far (rounds 1 through current - 1). Pairing functions use it for score, color history, opponent history, and float history.
For round-robin pairing, the games parameter is actually ignored — Berger tables are deterministic and don't depend on results. The parameter exists only for signature compatibility with swiss.

Two approaches considered for games format: Game[] with round field (current — one type across ecosystem, no conversion needed, round explicit per game) vs Game[][] where index is the round (cleaner, round is structural not data, but requires conversion between packages). Trade-off between ecosystem consistency and structural correctness.

Tiebreak packages can adapt to Game[][] format — progressive uses round order (iterates outer array by index, simpler than current), all other tiebreaks iterate all games flat (call games.flat() once). The Game interface drops the round field when using Game[][].

Switching to Game[][] affects 11 packages: 8 tiebreaks (Game drops round, signature Game[] to Game[][]), 2 pairing (swiss, round-robin — Game drops round, Game[] to Game[][], round parameter removed from pair()), 1 orchestrator (tournament — stores games as Game[][] internally)
@echecs/trf is a TRF parser package that is also affected by the Game[][] change — it would need to output games grouped by round

RoundResult interface in @echecs/trf: { color: 'b' | 'w' | '-'; opponentId: number | null; result: ResultCode; round: number } — it's per-player not per-game. Each player has a results: RoundResult[] array. This is player-centric (one row per player per round) vs Game which is game-centric (one entry per pairing).
RoundResult's round field is TRF's own concern and is not affected by the Game[][] change — TRF stores results player-centric while Game is game-centric, they are different shapes

TRF format encodes round results in player lines (001 records) at fixed column positions starting at 92, repeating every 10 columns. Each round block contains: opponent pairing number (4 chars), color (w/b/-), result code (1/0/=/+/-/H/F/U/Z). Round number is implicit from column position.

Round number can be inferred from array position — storing round on each Game is redundant. TRF uses positional columns, pairing systems are called once per round, tournament orchestrator manages rounds sequentially. In chess tournaments every player has a result for every round (even byes/forfeits), so games[2] = round 3 is always valid.

Swiss utilities use Game.round in three places: colorHistory (sort by round), matchCount (set of round numbers), matchColorHistory (group by round). All become simpler with Game[][] since round is the array index.

Backlog items from tiebreak session: 1) @echecs/tournament needs to absorb standings() and update to Game[][]/black/white. 2) Unplayed rounds management (FIDE 16) — adjusting scores for forfeit wins, requested byes, pairing-allocated byes not yet implemented in tiebreak packages. 3) @echecs/trf needs Game[][] and black/white migration.

FIDE C.07 Section 16 defines 5 categories of unplayed rounds for tiebreak calculations: 1) Pairing-allocated byes / full-point byes (16.2.1), 2) Forfeit wins (16.2.2), 3) Requested byes followed by at least one played round (16.2.3), 4) Forfeit losses (16.2.4), 5) Requested byes followed only by more unplayed rounds or in the last round (16.2.5). Each category is treated differently in tiebreak calculations.

The Game type needs to carry metadata about why a round was unplayed (bye type, forfeit, etc.) to implement FIDE 16. Currently Game { black, result, white } cannot distinguish between pairing-allocated byes, forfeit wins, requested byes, etc. — black: '' is the only bye representation.
FIDE 16 and tournament package update are intertwined — FIDE 16 requires Game to carry unplayed-round metadata, and tournament is where that metadata originates. Both should be done in one pass.

Proposed extending Result from numeric (0 | 0.5 | 1) to a string union that encodes both outcome and nature: 'win', 'loss', 'draw' (OTB), plus 'forfeit-win', 'forfeit-loss', 'half-point-bye', 'full-point-bye', 'pairing-allocated-bye', 'zero-point-bye'. Mirrors TRF result codes (1, 0, =, +, -, H, F, U, Z) but in readable form.
TRF result codes: 1 (win), 0 (loss), = (draw), + (forfeit win), - (forfeit loss), H (half-point bye), F (full-point bye), U (pairing-allocated bye), Z (zero-point bye)

Tiebreak packages need the extended Result type (FIDE 16 metadata about unplayed rounds) but pairing packages do not — Swiss pairing algorithms (FIDE C.04) don't care about why a previous result happened, they only use score, color history, and opponent history. A forfeit win and an OTB win both contribute 1 point for pairing purposes.

Game uses `kind` (not `type`) as the discriminant field for distinguishing played games from byes/forfeits — `kind` is the conventional TypeScript name for discriminated union fields and avoids collision with the `type` keyword

Game.kind values mapping to FIDE 16 categories: undefined (OTB game, 0/0.5/1 points), 'pairing-bye' (16.2.1, 1pt), 'full-bye' (16.2.1, 1pt), 'forfeit-win' (16.2.2, 1pt), 'half-bye' (16.2.3 or 16.2.5 depending on context, 0.5pt), 'forfeit-loss' (16.2.4, 0pt), 'zero-bye' (16.2.3 or 16.2.5 depending on context, 0pt). FIDE 16.2.3 vs 16.2.5 distinction depends on whether the player played again after the bye.

Classifying FIDE 16.2.3 vs 16.2.5 requires scanning the full tournament context — specifically whether the player returned to play after the bye. TRF provides raw bye types (H, F, U, Z) per round but does not pre-classify 16.2.3 vs 16.2.5. Tiebreak packages receiving Game[][] can scan subsequent rounds to make this determination themselves.

Game.kind stays simple with 6 values matching TRF codes (no pre-classification of 16.2.3 vs 16.2.5). Tiebreak functions scan Game[][] to determine whether a half-bye/zero-bye is 16.2.3 (player returned) or 16.2.5 (player did not return).

Every echecs package must contain a SPEC.md file in the root with the relevant FIDE specification. Some packages (swiss, elo, etc.) already have them, but the 8 tiebreak packages, round-robin, and tournament still need them.

GameKind type: 'forfeit-loss' | 'forfeit-win' | 'full-bye' | 'half-bye' | 'pairing-bye' | 'zero-bye'. Used as optional `kind` field on Game interface. undefined means OTB game. Maps 1:1 to TRF result codes.

Only Buchholz (FIDE 8) and Sonneborn-Berger (FIDE 9.1) are affected by FIDE 16 unplayed rounds logic — they are based on opponents' scores. The other 6 tiebreak packages (koya, progressive, direct-encounter, number-of-wins, average-rating, performance-rating) are unaffected — their calculations use the player's own record or ratings, not opponents' results.

No indirection — each entry file IS the implementation.

BYE_SENTINEL is '' (empty string) used to identify bye games in the echecs ecosystem. A bye game is always { black: '', white: 'PlayerX', result: 1 } — the player receiving the bye is always in the white field. This is an arbitrary convention from @echecs/swiss's assignBye function.

Byes don't affect color selection in Swiss pairing — color history is computed only from OTB games. Bye rounds are skipped when computing color preference. The white/black fields on a bye game are meaningless for pairing purposes.

Bye games now use { black: 'A', white: 'A' } (same player for both colors) instead of { black: '', white: 'A' }. Eliminates BYE_SENTINEL constant. Bye detection becomes g.black === g.white or g.kind !== undefined. Both fields always contain real player identifiers.

BYE_SENTINEL eliminated from entire echecs ecosystem. 9 of 11 packages updated (progressive and round-robin had no bye-related code). Current test counts: swiss 117, round-robin 40, tournament 39, 8 tiebreak packages 47 total. Bye format is now { black: 'A', white: 'A', kind: 'half-bye', result: 0.5 }.

FIDE endorsement requires FPC (checker) and RTG (generator) tools — FPC checks pairings, RTG generates test tournaments
FIDE endorsement threshold: fewer than 10 discrepancies across 5000 seeds between the candidate program and a reference pairing engine
bbpPairings is a reference FIDE-endorsed pairing engine used to validate candidate programs during endorsement testing
VCL19 is FIDE's 19-item verification checklist that must be completed for pairing program endorsement
FE-1 is the FIDE endorsement application form that must be filled in and submitted for program endorsement
C.04.A is the FIDE regulation section governing pairing program endorsement requirements
FIDE endorsement requires FPC to be publicly available
FIDE (Federation Internationale des Echecs) is the governing body for international chess that endorses pairing programs and sets tournament rules
FPC (FIDE Pairing Checker) is a tool that checks whether pairings comply with FIDE Swiss pairing rules
RTG (Random Tournament Generator) generates test tournaments for validating pairing programs during FIDE endorsement

FIDE endorsement is for a program, not a standalone engine or library. The endorsed list (Annex-3/FEP19) contains complete tools like Swiss-Manager, Vega, bbpPairings. However, bbpPairings set precedent for endorsing a headless pairing tool as long as it provides FPC + RTG + TRF16 support.
TRF16 (Tournament Report File format version 16) support is required for FIDE endorsement — pairing programs must read and write this format

VCL19 operational requirements beyond pairing rules: FIDE mode must be the default mode, default pairing system must be specified, must prohibit FIDE-banned practices, must handle adjourned/postponed games, must support rejection of incorrect results
C.04.3 is the FIDE Dutch System pairing rules — VCL19 items 1-18 map to specific C.04.3 articles covering initial order, p1 determination, scoregroups, and other pairing criteria
Annex-3/FEP19 is the FIDE list of endorsed pairing programs — includes Swiss-Manager, Vega, bbpPairings among others

VCL19 Section A — FIDE Mode Requirements (VCL.01-06): VCL.01 FIDE mode must be default operating mode; VCL.02 FIDE mode accessible by standard installation/invocation; VCL.03 default pairing system must be the endorsed one and clearly specified; VCL.04-06 cover multi-endorsement scenarios and mode switching

VCL.01-06 (FIDE Mode) are about a program, not a library. A headless pairing engine doesn't have modes or standard invocation like Swiss-Manager. The CLI tools (endorsement check, endorsement generate) effectively serve as the program interface for endorsement purposes.

Verification strategy for FIDE endorsement: hybrid approach — implicit proof where coverage is strong (RTG cross-validation), targeted tests where coverage is weak, and "not applicable" declarations where honest (e.g. GUI-specific VCL items for a headless library)

Current endorsement test results: 0 discrepancies across 5000 seeds and 1.9M pairings. Two-way cross-validation with bbpPairings (they check ours, we check theirs). This exceeds the A.7 requirement of <=10 discrepancies per 5000 seeds.
@echecs/endorsement is the package containing FPC and RTG tooling for FIDE endorsement testing. @echecs/swiss is the pairing engine library implementing FIDE Dutch System rules.

The program under FIDE endorsement is the @echecs ecosystem as a whole: @echecs/swiss (pairing engine), @echecs/tournament (tournament management), @echecs/trf (TRF16 I/O). @echecs/endorsement is the FPC+RTG tooling that proves compliance. Pairing system: FIDE Dutch (C.04.3).
VCL.01-06 (FIDE Mode) status: all pass by design — only one mode exists (always FIDE Dutch rules), standard installation via npm, endorsed for FIDE Dutch C.04.3, no other pairing systems to configure

VCL.07-10 (Pairing Requirements): VCL.07 proven by 5000-seed cross-validation (0 discrepancies); VCL.08 requires verifying pairing by pairing numbers not ratings; VCL.09 gap — need to verify Baku acceleration support (C.04.7); VCL.10 references C.04.5.1/C.04.7 — Baku acceleration is C.04.7
VCL.11-12 (Import/Export): VCL.11 TRF16 import handled by @echecs/trf, used by FPC; VCL.12 gap — TRF16 export needs testing for different scoring points and UTF-8 support

VCL.13-19 (Tournament Requirements): VCL.13 gap — unusual results (1/2-0, 0-1/2, 0-0 unforfeited) representable in TRF16 since each player has independent result codes; VCL.16 gap — needs verification; VCL.17 gap — needs verification

Five known VCL19 compliance gaps identified: VCL.09 (Baku acceleration), VCL.12 (TRF16 export with different scoring + UTF-8), VCL.13 (unusual results), VCL.16, VCL.17. These require upstream fixes in @echecs packages.
@echecs/tournament is the tournament management package in the @echecs ecosystem; @echecs/trf handles TRF16 format reading and writing

TRF16 result codes: 1 (win), 0 (loss), = (draw), + (win by forfeit), - (loss by forfeit), W (bye win), L (bye loss), D (bye draw), H (half-point bye), F (full-point bye), Z (zero-point bye), U (unplayed). Each player line has its own independent result code per round.
Baku acceleration (C.04.7) is a FIDE-approved acceleration system for Swiss tournaments — VCL.09 requires supporting it. Sometimes confused with C.04.5.1 (Baku system).
SPPC (Swiss Pairing Programs Committee) is the FIDE body that reviews endorsement applications and VCL19 compliance evidence

VCL19 compliance test plan covers all 19 items organized by task groups: Task 1 (VCL.01-06 FIDE mode), Task 2+ covers pairing requirements, import/export, and tournament requirements. VCL.02 (standard installation) and VCL.06 (FIDE label restriction) were initially missed in the plan and added during self-review.

VCL.05 compliance approach: @echecs/swiss PairOptions only exposes expectedRounds and trace — neither bypasses FIDE rules. The API surface doesn't offer bypass knobs, so VCL.05 (no mechanism to circumvent FIDE rules) passes by design. Compliance document explains this reasoning.
@echecs/trf parse() returns an empty tournament object for garbage/malformed input, not null. Tests adjusted to check for empty object instead of null return.
@echecs/tournament correct() requires a pair() call first to promote the round to completedRounds before correction can be applied. VCL.15 test adjusted for this workflow.
VCL.17 gap confirmed: @echecs/tournament does NOT emit a warning when awarding full-point byes. This is a known missing feature that needs upstream fix for endorsement compliance.

VCL19 compliance test suite final results: 14 test files, 66 tests, all passing. 18/19 VCL items pass. Only gap is VCL.17 (full-point bye deprecation warning not emitted by @echecs/tournament). Half-point byes work correctly.

The original docs/vcl19.md had incorrect mapping — it mapped VCL items to C.04.3 articles (custom interpretation). Was corrected to match actual VCL19 items: A. FIDE Mode (01-06), B. Pairing (07-10), C. Import/Export (11-12), D. Tournament (13-19).
FIDE rules require a deprecation warning when full-point byes are assigned to players. Half-point byes are the standard and don't require a warning. This is VCL.17 compliance requirement.
GitHub issue echecsjs/tournament#43 tracks the VCL.17 gap — @echecs/tournament needs to emit a deprecation warning when full-point byes are assigned

@echecs/tournament@3.1.1 fixed VCL.17: the deprecation warning for full-point byes is emitted in the pair() method when a bye has kind === 'full'. The standard Dutch pairing engine produces kind: 'pairing' byes — full-point byes come from pre-assigned byes, not pairing output. Warning uses a callback pattern: this.#r(`full-point bye for "${e.player}" is deprecated by FIDE...`)
VCL19 compliance: all 19/19 items now pass after @echecs/tournament@3.1.1 fixed VCL.17. 14 test files, 66 tests, all green. No remaining gaps.

FIDE endorsement submission requirements beyond VCL19: FE-1 application form must be filled in (software version, auto-test report numbers, signature). Per C.04.A A.7, application must reach SPPC secretariat at least 4 months before Congress. FPC+RTG tooling, 0 discrepancies across 5000 seeds, and VCL19 test suite are complete.

FIDE does not provide official test fixtures for endorsement. The procedure is entirely procedural: RTG generates 5000 tournaments, an endorsed FPC checks them (and vice versa). The 0-discrepancy cross-validation with bbpPairings IS the validation — no FIDE-supplied TRF files to run against.

FE-1 application draft created at docs/fe1-application.md. Needs: country field, signature/date. Email set to [something]@echecs.dev. FE-1 template at docs/fe1-template.md was for discrepancy reports, not the actual application.

FIDE endorsed programs list (FEP24 is current): Vega, SwissSys, Swiss Manager, Swiss Master, Swiss-Chess/WinSwiss, Chess Online. These are tournament programs, not engines. bbpPairings is an endorsed pairing engine (since Minsk 2018 FEP19) — different category from tournament programs.

2026 FIDE Congress: Samarkand, Uzbekistan, September 19-28, 2026 (alongside 46th Chess Olympiad Sept 15-27). Endorsement application deadline: 4 months before Congress = approximately May 19-28, 2026.

FIDE endorsement contacts: TEC Secretary is Hendrik Petrus Du Toit (secretary.tec@fide.com); SPP Department lead is Roberto Ricca (r.ricca-tec@rrweb.org). Roberto Ricca is also author of JaVaFo (endorsed pairing engine). FE-1 should go to both. Ricca handles endorsement applications directly.
JaVaFo is a FIDE-endorsed pairing engine authored by Roberto Ricca, who heads the FIDE SPP (Swiss Pairing Programs) department
FIDE C.04.A is the appendix for endorsement of a software program — it defines the requirements a Swiss pairing engine must meet to receive official FIDE endorsement.
@echecs/endorsement is a CLI package for FIDE Swiss Software Endorsement per C.04.A, providing tools to verify and test the @echecs/swiss Dutch pairing engine.
@echecs/swiss is the Dutch pairing engine in the @echecs ecosystem, implementing FIDE Dutch Swiss pairing rules.
bbpPairings is a reference FIDE-endorsed Swiss pairing program used as the benchmark for validating other pairing engines.
TRF16 is a tournament report file format used to describe Swiss chess tournaments — it encodes player data, pairings, and results for each round.
FPC (Free Pairings Checker) is a CLI tool that reads a TRF16 file, replays each round through a Swiss pairing engine, and compares the generated pairings against the recorded ones.
FIDE Dutch pairing system is the standard Swiss pairing algorithm endorsed by FIDE for Swiss-system chess tournaments.

The @echecs/swiss Dutch pairing engine is validated against bbpPairings using RTG (Random Tournament Generator) tournaments — achieving 49834/49854 perfect rounds (99.96%) across 5000 RTG tournaments with 0 cases where bbpPairings produces a better matching.
RTG (Random Tournament Generator) generates random Swiss chess tournaments for testing pairing engines against reference implementations.
@echecs/trf is a package in the @echecs ecosystem for reading and writing TRF (Tournament Report File) format data.
FIDE (Federation Internationale des Echecs) is the international chess governing body that sets rules and standards for chess software endorsement.
SPP (Swiss Pairing Programs) is a FIDE initiative at spp.fide.com that manages endorsement of Swiss pairing software programs.

FIDE Section 7.2.4 defines RTG (Random Tournament Generator) as a freely available tool embedded in the THP (Tournament Handling Platform), preferably run from a CLI, able to generate simulated tournaments based on various customisable parameters.

FIDE Section A.5 (from C.04.A procedure): RTG generates simulated tournaments producing a full TRF16 file for each. In all cases the RTG generates a tournament paired by a FIDE-endorsed program (the reference program). RTG can use any endorsed program — it is not tied to bbpPairings specifically.
VCL19 is the checklist used in the FIDE endorsement procedure for validating Swiss pairing software programs. It is part of the C.04.A appendix process.
THP (Tournament Handling Platform) is the FIDE platform that embeds tools like RTG for tournament management and pairing software endorsement.
FIDE 2026 Handbook Section 7 updated the terminology and requirements for Swiss pairing software endorsement — it replaces and modernizes the older C.04.A procedure language.
For @echecs/endorsement FPC (check command): reads a TRF16 file, replays rounds through @echecs/swiss/dutch, and compares against stored pairings — no external binary (bbpPairings) needed.

RTG tournament generation for FIDE endorsement requires an external FIDE-endorsed reference program to generate the pairings. The RTG can use any endorsed program — it is not restricted to bbpPairings.
rtg-compare.mts is an existing script in the @echecs workspace that depends on an external bbpPairings binary for both RTG generation and as the reference for comparison.

PTC (Pairings and Tie-breaks Checker) is a FIDE tool defined in 2026 Handbook Section 7.2.3, embedded within a THP, that verifies both pairings and tie-breaks using the program's built-in pairing and tie-break engine. It replaces/extends the older FPC concept.

@echecs/endorsement check command verifies pairings only for the initial release — not tie-breaks. Rationale: (1) rtg-compare.mts already validates pairings at 99.96%, (2) tie-break verification depends on @echecs/tournament which is a separate concern, (3) FIDE procedure A.7 describes FPC+RTG for 5000-tournament automated verification which is pairings not tie-breaks, (4) VCL19 checklist items are about pairing correctness. Tie-break checking can be added later as a --tie-breaks flag.
FIDE procedure A.7 describes using FPC + RTG for the 5000-tournament automated verification step in the endorsement process — this step focuses on pairing correctness, not tie-break standings.
@echecs/tournament is a package in the @echecs ecosystem that handles tournament standings and tie-break logic.

@echecs/endorsement is a standalone package at endorsement/ in the workspace. Runtime dependencies: @echecs/swiss (pairing engine), @echecs/tournament (shared types — the interface swiss implements), @echecs/trf (TRF16 parse and stringify). No other runtime deps.

@echecs/endorsement CLI uses node:util.parseArgs for argument parsing — no CLI framework dependency. Plain stdout for output, no colors, no spinners. Aligns with the workspace convention of zero unnecessary runtime deps.

@echecs/tournament defines the shared types used by the ecosystem: Player, Game, Pairing, PairingResult, Bye, GameKind. It is the "interface" that @echecs/swiss implements. Swiss has tournament as a peer dep.

@echecs/endorsement ships two commands via a single binary entry point: `echecs-endorsement check` (FPC — replays rounds and compares pairings) and `echecs-endorsement generate` (RTG — generates random tournaments).

Core module structure for @echecs/endorsement: src/check.ts (FPC logic), src/generate.ts (RTG logic), src/types.ts (shared types). Key functions extracted from rtg-compare.mts: trfToSwiss() converts parsed TRF to Player[]+Game[][] for pair(), extractRoundPairings() gets expected pairings from TRF, extractAbsentPlayers() finds pre-assigned absent players.

trfToSwiss(raw: string) function converts a parsed TRF tournament into the Player[] + Game[][] structure that the pair() function expects. It handles bye classification (F/H/Z/U), forfeits, and double forfeits.
Bye types in TRF/Swiss pairing: F (full-point bye), H (half-point bye), Z (zero-point bye), U (unpaired/absent). These affect pairing and scoring differently.
endorsement/docs/journal.md is a chronological log tracking the FIDE endorsement process: decisions made, validation results, FIDE submission status, and discrepancy analysis.

@echecs workspace conventions are documented in root AGENTS.md — covers package structure, dev deps, config files, CI workflows, and initial release protocol. Each package follows consistent patterns for tsconfig, vitest, build tooling, etc.

@echecs/endorsement implementation references: ../swiss/scripts/rtg-compare.mts (existing FPC logic to refactor into src/check.ts), ../swiss/src/dutch.ts (the pair() function signature), ../swiss/src/types.ts (re-exported tournament types), ../trf/ (TRF16 parsing/writing). Design spec at endorsement/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-01-endorsement-design.md.

@echecs/endorsement implementation order: (1) package scaffolding (package.json, tsconfig, etc.), (2) src/types.ts with CheckResult/GenerateResult types, (3) src/check.ts refactored from rtg-compare.mts, (4) src/generate.ts for RTG, (5) src/cli.ts as the bin entry, (6) tests, (7) endorsement/docs/journal.md with first entry.

rtg-compare.mts lives at swiss/scripts/rtg-compare.mts — it contains the existing FPC logic (trfToSwiss, extractRoundPairings, extractAbsentPlayers functions) that gets refactored into @echecs/endorsement src/check.ts.
dutch.ts lives at swiss/src/dutch.ts — exports the pair() function which is the main Swiss Dutch pairing algorithm entry point in @echecs/swiss.
